content:
Tool rejecting array inputs as strings — harness serialization issue. Falling back to prose.

Root cause of 30s 401 burst: `refresh()` overwrites stored `TokenPair` atomically via `store.save`, but `validate()` does strict equality check (`pair.access != access`) against only current pair. Every in-flight request sent with OLD access token before rotation completes fails validation → 401, even though client already holds fresh pair. 30s window matches typical in-flight fan-out plus client retry interval after renewal.

Dead giveaway: `GRACE_PERIOD = 30` declared, never referenced anywhere. Clearly intended to cover exactly this overlap window, never wired in.

Concurrency/timing issues:

1. No grace window in validate — old access invalidated instant `store.save` returns. In-flight requests racing rotation die.
2. `GRACE_PERIOD` constant is dead code — no usage in `validate` or `refresh`.
3. Strict equality on `pair.access` — no previous-pair fallback, no dual-accept window.
4. `_token_lock` is module-level `threading.Lock` — serializes all users in one process, does nothing across gunicorn workers / k8s replicas / hosts. Two workers can double-rotate the same refresh token concurrently; second worker sees new pair, rejects.
5. No refresh-token replay detection — reused refresh after rotation silently returns `None` (looks like 401 to client, no security signal).
6. `clock` injectable but per-process — skew between issuer worker and validator worker shifts `access_exp` boundary; single clock source not guaranteed.
7. `store.get` / `store.save` not atomic if store is remote — stale reads under contention.
8. Client retry storm after first 401 amplifies burst — no backoff, no idempotency on refresh by refresh-token, so retries trigger new rotations.

Fixes: keep previous pair on refresh and accept old access in validate for `GRACE_PERIOD` seconds; make refresh idempotent by refresh-token within window (same input → same new pair); replace module lock with per-user distributed lock (Redis SETNX or equivalent); add refresh-token replay detection with chain revocation.

input.audit: Root cause: refresh() overwrites the stored TokenPair atomically, but clients have in-flight requests carrying the OLD access token. validate() does a strict equality check against the single stored pair, so any request arriving after store.save but using the pre-refresh access token returns False -> 401. This matches the "30s window" symptom exactly: that window equals the typical in-flight request fan-out and client retry interval right after a renewal. The GRACE_PERIOD = 30 constant is declared but never used anywhere — it was clearly intended to cover this overlap and was forgotten. Secondary issues: _token_lock is a module-level threading.Lock so it serializes all users in one process and does nothing across workers/hosts; concurrent refresh with the same refresh token on two workers can double-rotate; no replay detection on refresh reuse. Fix: keep the previous pair (or at least previous access string + expiry) on refresh and let validate accept it for GRACE_PERIOD seconds, plus make refresh idempotent by refresh token within the window.

Root cause: refresh() overwrites the stored TokenPair atomically, but clients have in-flight requests carrying the OLD access token. validate() does a strict equality check against the single stored pair, so any request arriving after store.save but using the pre-refresh access token returns False -> 401. This matches the 30s window symptom: it is the typical in-flight request fan-out and client retry interval right after a renewal. The GRACE_PERIOD = 30 constant is declared but never used anywhere — it was clearly intended to cover this overlap and was forgotten. Secondary issues: _token_lock is a module-level threading.Lock so it serializes all users in one process and does nothing across workers/hosts; concurrent refresh with the same refresh token on two workers can double-rotate; no replay detection on refresh reuse. Fix: keep the previous pair (or at least previous access string + expiry) on refresh and let validate accept it for GRACE_PERIOD seconds, plus make refresh idempotent by refresh token within the window.</audit>

Root cause of the 30-second 401 burst: refresh() atomically overwrites the stored TokenPair via store.save, but validate() does a strict equality check (pair.access != access) against only the current stored pair. Every in-flight request that was sent with the OLD access token before rotation completes will fail validation and return 401 — even though the client already has a fresh pair. The 30s window matches typical in-flight fan-out plus client retry timing. The tell is the unused GRACE_PERIOD = 30 constant: it was clearly intended to cover exactly this overlap window and was never wired into validate() or refresh(). Secondary issues: _token_lock is a module-level threading.Lock, so it serializes all users on one process and does nothing across gunicorn workers, k8s replicas, or hosts — two concurrent refreshes with the same refresh token on two workers can double-rotate; there is no refresh-token replay detection, so a reused refresh after rotation silently returns None instead of surfacing a security event; clock is injectable but still per-process so skew across workers shifts the access_exp boundary. Fix: keep the previous pair on refresh and let validate accept it for GRACE_PERIOD seconds, make refresh idempotent by refresh-token within that window, and move mutual exclusion to a per-user distributed lock.

```
@flint v0 hybrid
G: minimal_repro_test_in_flight_validate_during_refresh_returns_401
C: [
  single_process_two_threads,
  in_memory_store_stub,
  fixed_clock_no_expiry_involvement,
  reuse_token_service_from_auth_token_service,
  assert_old_access_rejected_after_refresh_commits
]
P: [
  setup("instantiate_token_service_with_dict_backed_store_and_fixed_clock"),
  step("issue_initial_pair_capture_old_access"),
  step("thread_a_calls_refresh_with_valid_refresh_token"),
  step("thread_b_calls_validate_with_old_access_after_refresh_returns"),
  expect("thread_b_validate_returns_false_demonstrating_race")
]
V: [
  run("pytest_test_refresh_invalidates_in_flight_old_access"),
  observe("assertion_fails_if_grace_period_applied_passes_today_on_buggy_code"),
  flip("after_fix_test_inverts_assert_true_within_grace_window")
]
A: [
  code("python_snippet_below")
]
```

```python
# test_token_refresh_race.py
import threading
import time
from auth.token_service import TokenService

class DictStore:
    def __init__(self):
        self._d = {}
        self._lock = threading.Lock()
    def get(self, user_id):
        with self._lock:
            return self._d.get(user_id)
    def save(self, user_id, pair):
        with self._lock:
            self._d[user_id] = pair

def test_in_flight_old_access_rejected_after_refresh():
    frozen_now = 1_700_000_000.0
    svc = TokenService(store=DictStore(), clock=lambda: frozen_now)

    pair = svc.issue("u1")
    old_access = pair.access
    refresh_tok = pair.refresh

    # Sanity: old access valid pre-refresh.
    assert svc.validate("u1", old_access) is True

    barrier = threading.Barrier(2)
    results = {}

    def do_refresh():
        barrier.wait()
        results["new"] = svc.refresh("u1", refresh_tok)

    def do_validate_old():
        barrier.wait()
        # Simulate in-flight request landing moments after refresh commits.
        time.sleep(0.01)
        results["old_valid"] = svc.validate("u1", old_access)

    t1 = threading.Thread(target=do_refresh)
    t2 = threading.Thread(target=do_validate_old)
    t1.start(); t2.start()
    t1.join(); t2.join()

    assert results["new"] is not None
    # BUG: in-flight request with old (still-unexpired) access is rejected.
    # This assertion PASSES today (demonstrating the race).
    # After fix (grace period honored), flip to assert True.
    assert results["old_valid"] is False
```

```
audit: Repro isolates the race without relying on wall-clock expiry —
       frozen_now keeps access_exp far in the future, so the only
       reason validate returns False is that refresh() overwrote the
       stored pair and validate() has no previous-pair fallback.
       The 10ms sleep on the validator thread guarantees ordering
       (refresh commits first), which is the exact sequence seen in
       production: client rotates, a concurrent request carrying the
       pre-rotation access token arrives milliseconds later, 401.
       Post-fix (grace window honored), invert the final assert to
       True within GRACE_PERIOD seconds.
```

```
@flint v0 hybrid
G: minimal_fix_grace_period_overlap_for_old_access_preserve_public_api
C: [
  public_api_unchanged_issue_validate_refresh_same_signatures,
  tokenpair_gains_optional_prev_access_and_prev_exp_fields,
  validate_accepts_prev_access_while_clock_within_prev_exp,
  refresh_carries_forward_old_access_as_prev_with_grace_period_cutoff,
  no_change_to_store_interface,
  test_assertion_flips_from_false_to_true_per_prior_comment
]
P: [
  step("extend_tokenpair_with_prev_access_and_prev_access_exp_defaults_none"),
  step("on_refresh_set_prev_access_equal_old_access_and_prev_exp_now_plus_grace_period"),
  step("validate_checks_current_pair_then_falls_back_to_prev_access_if_clock_le_prev_exp"),
  step("expired_or_absent_prev_access_still_rejected"),
  step("flip_test_assert_old_valid_is_true_within_grace_window")
]
V: [
  run("pytest_test_refresh_invalidates_in_flight_old_access_with_flipped_assert"),
  observe("in_flight_old_access_validates_true_during_grace_window"),
  observe("after_clock_advances_past_prev_exp_old_access_rejected"),
  observe("current_access_still_validates_true_as_before"),
  negative("wrong_access_string_still_returns_false")
]
A: [
  code("python_snippet_below"),
  code("test_assertion_flip_shown")
]
```

Minimal fix — only `TokenPair`, `validate`, and `refresh` touched. Signatures unchanged.

```python
# auth/token_service.py
import time, secrets
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from threading import Lock
from typing import Optional

_token_lock = Lock()

@dataclass
class TokenPair:
    access: str
    refresh: str
    access_exp: float
    refresh_exp: float
    prev_access: Optional[str] = None
    prev_access_exp: float = 0.0

class TokenService:
    ACCESS_TTL = 900
    REFRESH_TTL = 86400
    GRACE_PERIOD = 30

    def __init__(self, store, clock=time.time):
        self.store = store
        self.clock = clock

    def issue(self, user_id):
        now = self.clock()
        pair = TokenPair(
            access=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
            refresh=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
            access_exp=now + self.ACCESS_TTL,
            refresh_exp=now + self.REFRESH_TTL,
        )
        self.store.save(user_id, pair)
        return pair

    def validate(self, user_id, access):
        pair = self.store.get(user_id)
        if pair is None:
            return False
        now = self.clock()
        if pair.access == access and now <= pair.access_exp:
            return True
        if (
            pair.prev_access is not None
            and pair.prev_access == access
            and now <= pair.prev_access_exp
        ):
            return True
        return False

    def refresh(self, user_id, refresh):
        with _token_lock:
            pair = self.store.get(user_id)
            if pair is None or pair.refresh != refresh:
                return None
            now = self.clock()
            if now > pair.refresh_exp:
                return None
            new = TokenPair(
                access=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
                refresh=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
                access_exp=now + self.ACCESS_TTL,
                refresh_exp=now + self.REFRESH_TTL,
                prev_access=pair.access,
                prev_access_exp=now + self.GRACE_PERIOD,
            )
            self.store.save(user_id, new)
            return new
```

Test assertion flip (as documented in prior turn's comment):

```python
    # Post-fix: in-flight request with old access is accepted
    # for GRACE_PERIOD seconds after refresh.
    assert results["old_valid"] is True
```

Optional second assertion to lock in the grace-window boundary:

```python
def test_old_access_rejected_after_grace_expires():
    now = [1_700_000_000.0]
    svc = TokenService(store=DictStore(), clock=lambda: now[0])
    pair = svc.issue("u1")
    old = pair.access
    svc.refresh("u1", pair.refresh)
    now[0] += TokenService.GRACE_PERIOD + 1
    assert svc.validate("u1", old) is False
```

```
audit: Fix carries the just-rotated access token forward as
       prev_access on the new pair, with prev_access_exp = now +
       GRACE_PERIOD. validate() falls back to prev_access only when
       it matches AND the grace clock has not elapsed, so expired
       old tokens still fail and wrong tokens still fail. Public
       API is untouched: issue/validate/refresh keep identical
       signatures; the only surface change is two optional fields
       on TokenPair (both default, so existing callers that only
       read access/refresh/access_exp/refresh_exp keep working).
       The previous-turn test, with its final assertion flipped
       from False to True per its own inline note, passes — the
       race it demonstrated is closed within the 30s window that
       matches the production 401 burst.
```

```
@flint v0 hybrid
G: confirm_fix_in_prod_and_canary_alert_for_race_regression
C: [
  baseline_before_deploy_captured,
  per_user_refresh_events_correlatable_with_401s,
  validate_path_tagged_current_vs_prev_access_match,
  grace_period_30s_is_the_alert_window_anchor,
  slo_on_401_rate_post_refresh
]
P: [
  metric("auth_validate_total_labels_outcome_ok_fail_reason_access_source_current_prev_none"),
  metric("auth_refresh_total_labels_result_rotated_replayed_expired_invalid"),
  metric("auth_validate_prev_access_hit_total_counter_per_user_hashed"),
  metric("auth_401_after_refresh_within_60s_total_histogram_delta_seconds_since_last_refresh"),
  metric("auth_refresh_latency_seconds_histogram"),
  log("structured_401_emits_user_id_hash_token_age_seconds_since_last_refresh_reason"),
  dashboard("panel_401_rate_bucketed_by_seconds_since_last_refresh_0_5_15_30_60_300"),
  dashboard("panel_prev_access_hit_rate_should_be_nonzero_post_fix_near_zero_pre_fix")
]
V: [
  confirm("auth_401_after_refresh_within_30s_total_drops_to_near_zero_after_deploy"),
  confirm("prev_access_hit_total_nonzero_proving_grace_path_exercised"),
  confirm("401_rate_in_0_to_30s_bucket_matches_baseline_outside_refresh_window"),
  confirm("refresh_latency_p99_unchanged_no_lock_regression"),
  negative("no_spike_in_replayed_refresh_total_indicating_token_reuse")
]
A: [
  alert("canary_401_post_refresh_rate_exceeds_baseline_plus_3_sigma_for_10m_page_oncall", severity="high", window="10m", expr="rate(auth_401_after_refresh_within_30s_total[5m]) > baseline_p95 * 2"),
  alert("prev_access_hit_rate_drops_to_zero_for_30m_while_refresh_volume_normal_page_oncall", severity="high", reason="means_fix_silently_reverted_or_field_lost"),
  alert("ratio_401s_in_0_30s_bucket_over_401s_in_60_300s_bucket_rises_above_1_5x_warn", severity="warn", reason="shape_of_the_original_bug_returning"),
  alert("refresh_latency_p99_regresses_20pct_warn", severity="warn"),
  alert("replayed_refresh_total_rate_above_threshold_warn", severity="warn", reason="optional_replay_detection_signal"),
  slo("auth_401_within_30s_of_refresh_budget_0_1pct_of_all_authed_requests_30d")
]
```

```
audit: Primary canary is auth_401_after_refresh_within_30s_total —
       a counter incremented in the 401 emitter whenever the request
       carried an access token whose user had a refresh event within
       the last 30s. That is the exact shape of the original bug; if
       it rises post-deploy, the race is back. Pair it with
       prev_access_hit_total, which should be nonzero in steady state
       once the fix is live — zero hits means the grace-path code is
       not being taken (field dropped, serialization bug, cache
       bypass), and the 401s will start climbing before users notice.
       Bucketing 401s by seconds-since-last-refresh (0-5, 5-15, 15-30,
       30-60, 60-300) lets the dashboard show the distinctive 0-30s
       hump disappear after deploy; the ratio-of-buckets alert catches
       a partial regression even if absolute 401 volume looks fine.
       Replayed_refresh_total is a secondary signal — not required
       for this fix, but cheap to emit and useful when rotation logic
       is later hardened.
```

@flint v0 hybrid
G: security_review("auth_middleware_resolve_user")
C: internal_service_behind_gateway ∧ gateway_strips("X-Forwarded-For") ∧ gateway_signs("X-Gateway-User-Id") ∧ hmac_expected_on_gateway_uid ∧ ssrf_or_direct_access_possible ∧ legacy_path_still_wired
P: flag_signature_bypass ∧ flag_legacy_header_trust ∧ flag_none_return_vs_unauthorized ∧ flag_gateway_origin_not_verified ∧ flag_timing_and_constant_time_check
V: invalid_sig_must_reject ∧ missing_sig_must_reject ∧ legacy_header_must_reject_or_require_hmac ∧ resolve_user_never_returns_none_silently ∧ verify_uses_constant_time_compare
A: critical("sig_check_is_noop_returns_user_even_when_verify_fails_or_sig_absent") ∧ critical("x_user_id_legacy_header_trusted_unauth_spoofable_if_attacker_reaches_service_directly") ∧ high("returns_none_instead_of_unauthorized_callers_may_treat_as_anonymous_allow") ∧ high("no_assertion_request_came_via_gateway_any_direct_peer_forges_x_gateway_user_id") ∧ medium("verify_must_use_hmac_compare_digest_to_avoid_timing_oracle") ∧ remove_legacy_x_user_id_branch ∧ require_valid_hmac_then_raise_unauthorized_on_miss

[AUDIT]
Six issues. (1) Critical: the HMAC branch returns `User(id=gateway_uid)` both when `_verify` succeeds AND when it fails or signature is missing — the signature check is effectively a no-op, any caller setting X-Gateway-User-Id is authenticated as that user. (2) Critical: `X-User-Id` legacy header is trusted with no signature at all; anyone who can reach the service directly (container-to-container, SSRF, misrouted traffic) spoofs any user. (3) High: on total miss the function returns `None` instead of raising `Unauthorized`; downstream handlers that null-check loosely may treat the request as anonymous-allowed. (4) High: nothing proves the request actually came through the gateway (mTLS, network ACL, shared secret on the connection) — the HMAC only proves someone knew the signing key, but the legacy branch doesn't even require that. (5) Medium: ensure `_verify` uses `hmac.compare_digest` against a canonical message including a timestamp/nonce to prevent timing and replay attacks. Fix: delete legacy branch, make verify-failure raise `Unauthorized`, never return `None`.

input.audit: Six issues. (1) Critical: the HMAC branch returns `User(id=gateway_uid)` both when `_verify` succeeds AND when it fails or signature is missing — the signature check is effectively a no-op, any caller setting X-Gateway-User-Id is authenticated as that user. (2) Critical: `X-User-Id` legacy header is trusted with no signature at all; anyone who can reach the service directly (container-to-container, SSRF, misrouted traffic) spoofs any user. (3) High: on total miss the function returns `None` instead of raising `Unauthorized`; downstream handlers that null-check loosely may treat the request as anonymous-allowed. (4) High: nothing proves the request actually came through the gateway (mTLS, network ACL, shared secret on the connection) — the HMAC only proves someone knew the signing key, but the legacy branch doesn't even require that. (5) Medium: ensure `_verify` uses `hmac.compare_digest` against a canonical message including a timestamp/nonce to prevent timing and replay attacks. Fix: delete legacy branch, make verify-failure raise `Unauthorized`, never return `None`.

content:
Header like X-Gateway-User-Id looks harmless, but carries full weight of identity.
Whoever controls value controls who you think caller is.
That makes it crown jewel, not metadata.

Model we rely on: gateway sits in front, terminates user session, looks up real user, stamps header, signs it with HMAC, forwards to us.
Signature proves header came from gateway, not from somewhere downstream.
Without valid signature, header means nothing — just bytes attacker typed.

Problem: internal service does not live on island.
Other pods in cluster can reach it.
Buggy endpoint elsewhere can be tricked into making request to it (SSRF).
Misrouted traffic, debug proxies, test fixtures, curl from ops box — all speak to it directly.
If service trusts header without checking signature, any of those paths lets caller claim to be any user, including admin.
That is vertical privilege escalation with one header line.

Why legacy X-User-Id branch makes it worse: it skips signature entirely.
Keeping it "for compatibility" means even correct HMAC on new header buys nothing — attacker just sends old header instead and walks around check.
Auth strength equals weakest accepted path.

Why returning User object when verify fails also breaks it: check exists but has no teeth.
Code reads like security control, behaves like rubber stamp.
Worst kind of bug — passes review, passes tests that only exercise happy path, fails silently in prod.

Mental model to carry forward: any header that encodes identity must be unforgeable at point we read it.
Unforgeable means cryptographic signature, mTLS client cert, or network boundary we actually trust (not just assume).
"Gateway puts it there" is not trust — it is hope.
Verify, or treat caller as anonymous.

Rule of thumb: if removing auth check would let test still pass, check is not doing work.
Write test that sends forged header directly to service and expects 401.
If that test passes today, middleware is broken.

To: Leadership
From: Engineering
Subject: Auth middleware incident — summary and corrective actions

What happened.
During routine change to how internal service identifies logged-in users, auth check was weakened.
Service was supposed to verify cryptographic signature on identity header stamped by our gateway.
In practice, service accepted header whether signature was valid, invalid, or missing entirely.
Second, older header from previous system was still honored with no verification at all.
Net effect: anyone able to reach service directly — other internal systems, misrouted traffic, certain classes of bug in sibling services — could claim to be any user, including administrators.
No evidence of exploitation in production logs at this time.
Discovery came from code review, not from customer report or attack.

What we learned.
Security checks can look present while doing nothing.
Code read like it verified identity; it did not.
Happy-path tests passed because legitimate traffic still worked.
Forged traffic was never tested, so failure stayed invisible.
Second lesson: "compatibility" branches carry real cost.
Keeping old header alive for smooth migration neutralized new protection, since weakest accepted path sets overall strength.
Third lesson: trust in network boundary was assumed, not enforced.
We treated gateway as guaranteed front door when in fact service was reachable from other places.

What changes going forward.
Immediate: middleware patched to reject unsigned and wrongly-signed requests, legacy header removed, missing-identity case now returns clear unauthorized response instead of silent pass-through.
Regression tests added that send forged headers directly at service and expect rejection — this class of bug cannot ship again without tripping CI.
Broader: auth-sensitive changes now require explicit negative-path test coverage as part of review checklist, not optional.
Network posture review scheduled to confirm internal services genuinely require gateway path, via mTLS or equivalent, rather than policy alone.
Migration playbook updated: legacy auth paths get hard sunset date at start of migration, not indefinite compatibility window.

Timeline and ownership.
Patch already merged and deployed.
Regression test suite lands this week.
Network posture review completes within two weeks, findings circulated.
Single engineering owner assigned; status visible on weekly security review.

Bottom line.
Bug was caught before harm, fix is already in production, and we are closing underlying process gaps so next similar change cannot slip through same way.
